meta.title: Mercedes A-Class W169 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes A-Class W169 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes A-Class W169 — from the 1.5-litre M266 petrol to the 2.0-litre OM640 turbodiesel, plus the rare A200 Turbo. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the sandwich‑concept packaging that makes every repair a specialist job. Backed by 250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes A-Class W169 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W169 hatchback — from the entry‑level 1.5‑litre petrol to the 193bhp A200 Turbo, plus the OM640 diesel family that powered the majority of UK fleet sales.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All seven engines are in Case B — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for any Mercedes M266 or OM640 family. Ratings are derived from the Known Failure Patterns table in EM_Proprietary_Data and cross‑referenced against specialist/owner sources. The OM640 diesel family (82–140 bhp) is widely regarded as the more durable option; the M266 petrols are simple, low‑stress units with few major failure modes, though the A200 Turbo's added complexity drops its rating to ★★★☆☆. All ratings are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

overview.intro: The W169 is the second generation of the Mercedes‑Benz A‑Class, launched in September 2004 as the successor to the W168. It introduced a longer wheelbase, a choice of five‑door hatchback (W169) or three‑door Coupé (C169) body styles, and a completely new engine line‑up: the M266 petrol family (1.5L, 1.7L and 2.0L) and the OM640 turbodiesel family (2.0L in three power levels). The W169 retained Mercedes' sandwich‑concept safety architecture, with the engine and transmission inclined at up to 59° — a design that improved crash safety but made every repair a specialist job. A mid‑cycle facelift in 2008 brought revised styling, updated Euro 5 emissions compliance on later models, and the discontinuation of some early‑spec variants.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W169 (shared with C169 three‑door Coupé) • Predecessor: W168 • Successor: W176 • Sibling body styles in this generation: C169 (three‑door Coupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The A‑Class W169 generated an estimated 250+ UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED — PLACEHOLDER], concentrated on the A180 CDI and A200 CDI diesels as these ageing hatchbacks exit affordable‑warranty age and enter high‑mileage ownership.

items.quote: The sweet spot of the OM640 diesel family — 109 bhp, 250 Nm of torque from just 1,600 rpm, and no catastrophic failure modes. Simple common‑rail injection, robust chain‑driven valvetrain, and the same Euro 4 compliance that kept it in production for the full W169 lifecycle.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy without the complexity of the A200 CDI's higher boost or the entry‑level A160 CDI's sluggishness.

items.quote: The 1.7‑litre naturally aspirated petrol — 116 bhp, simple SOHC 8‑valve design, no turbo, no direct injection. It's the most honest engine in the W169 range: smooth, reliable, and cheap to maintain.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a straightforward petrol hatchback without turbo or diesel complications.

items.quote: The only forced‑induction petrol in the W169 range. 193 bhp from a 2.0‑litre turbo sounds appealing, but the added complexity — turbocharger, intercooler, higher boost — introduces failure modes the naturally aspirated M266s simply don't have. Specialist parts are harder to source, and the engine was only fitted to about 2% of W169s.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who understand that 193 bhp in a small hatchback comes with a maintenance premium.

items.quote: The highest‑output OM640 diesel — 140 bhp, more boost, more stress. When the turbo or injectors fail on this variant, the repair bill is significantly higher than the lower‑output OM640.940 or .941.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the most powerful diesel W169 and are prepared for the higher running costs.

items.quote: The entry‑level diesel — 82 bhp, 184 Nm, and the cheapest recon cost of any W169 diesel. It's slow, but it's honest, and the low purchase price of an A160 CDI combined with cheap replacement economics makes it the best value proposition in the range.
items.whoItsFor: budget‑conscious buyers who prioritise running costs over performance.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre naturally aspirated petrol — 136 bhp, no turbo, no direct injection. It's the quiet achiever of the W169 range: more power than the A170, but without the A200 Turbo's complexity or the diesels' DPF and injector concerns.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best naturally aspirated petrol W169 and don't need a diesel's fuel economy.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Overlooked Gem — more power, no turbo hassle
Best Diesel — sweet spot of the range
Most Expensive — more boost, more risk

economicsRule.title: The W169 rule
economicsRule.text: A £1,800–£3,200 OM640 recon against a £2,500–£5,000 W169 sits at 36–128% of the car's value. On a sub‑£3,000 W169, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £5,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 36–64% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W169 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M266.920 — cheap coil packs, no complex systems, lowest recon cost.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W169 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M266.980 A200 Turbo — turbo and intercooler repairs add up fast; 2% market share means parts are harder to source.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W169 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: A170 (M266.940) — petrol simplicity, 116 bhp, low running costs, and no DPF or injector concerns.

problems.title: M266 Petrol Coil Pack & Ignition Failure
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of misfire or rough idle, get the coil packs inspected immediately — a £150–£300 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £900–£1,800 diagnostic and replacement that follows if ignored. On a W169 worth under £3,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

problems.title: OM640 Injector Wear & DPF Clogging
problems.recommendation: On any OM640 W169 over 80,000 miles with no documented injector service history, budget for injector inspection — £400–£1,000 now is far cheaper than the £1,500–£2,800 replacement that follows a failed injector that has contaminated the DPF or damaged the turbo.

problems.title: W169 Sandwich‑Concept Labour Access
problems.recommendation: Always factor in the W169 labour premium before committing to any engine repair. A quote that seems "reasonable" for an A‑Class is often 40–80% higher than the same job on a C‑Class or E‑Class simply because of the sandwich‑concept access constraints. Get a specialist W169 quote before approving any work.

replacementCosts.h2: W169 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline — no Engine Page exists yet for any M266 or OM640 family. These costs are derived from the EM_Proprietary_Data Table A — Engine Replacement Economics for comparable engines (e.g., Renault K9K 1.5 dCi, Ford DV6 1.6 TDCi), adjusted for the W169's sandwich‑concept labour premium and marked [MB-QUOTE] accordingly. All figures are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W169 five‑door hatchback only. C169 three‑door Coupé shares all these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since labour access varies slightly by body style and the C169's sportier positioning attracts a different ownership profile.
